text: Severe Endometriosis Intrinsic Ureteral Disease We describe a thirty years old woman with primary infertility, right back lower pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia and dyscaesia. The urography shows the uretero-edonephrosis on the right side. The MRI revealed the stenosis of the ureter at the level of right ovarian fossa. The renal scintigraphy showed a forty percent kidney function on the right side.

During the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon uses bipolar cautery to detach the bowel from the abdominal wall, carefully following the white line to avoid entering the retroperitoneal space. The bowel is then fixed to the anterior abdominal wall using a T-lift device, providing access to the left parietal fossa.
The surgeon gently manipulates the left ovary, which is adhered to the bowel, using traction to separate it without electrosurgery.

The surgeon uses a D-lift to fix the left ovary onto the bottom wall. The left cloretal fossa is then opened using a bipolar device and irrigation, with traction and counter-traction movements. Bubbles from the neomodissection are visible, and careful attention is paid to the surrounding nerves.

The surgeon opens the right parietal fossa to isolate the rectum, performing a careful bilateral dissection to avoid damage to vessels or nerves. The rectovaginal nodule is approached with precision, cautiously entering the rectovaginal space to prevent dendrogenic bowel injury.
The surgeon uses scissors to carefully open the rectovaginal space, pulling granularly and downward to expose the area, leaving the nodule attached to the posterior vagina.

The surgeon performs ureterolysis to clearly identify the ureter and the site of endometriosis-induced stenosis and compression. The surrounding endometriosis is visibly causing retraction and infiltration of the ureter wall, while efforts are made to preserve the ureter's vascularization.

The surgeon uses a monocular hook to remove the retrovaginal endometriotic nodule, opening the vagina and employing a grasper with bipolar capability for extraction. A glove with gauzes is inserted into the vagina to prevent gas leakage during the procedure.

The surgeon closes the vaginal incision using a continuous polyfilament absorbable suture performed intercorporeally. The integrity of the bowel is tested by injecting air into the rectum while saline is present in the pelvic cavity, confirming no perforation.

Following urologic evaluation, the surgeon performs a uretero-uretero anastomosis. A DJ stent is introduced using a cystoscopic approach. The segment of the ureter affected by endometriosis is excised, and the two ends of the ureter are sutured together using interrupted monofilament absorbable sutures. The removed ureteric specimen is analyzed, revealing intrinsic endometriosis.
